item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I'm honest, this just doesn't do it for me. I get that giving away whatever created the protocol would kinda defeat the point, but (with few exceptions), I read the SCPs rather than tales to see SCPs rather than just a collection of odd after effects. Sometimes I make exceptions for particularly interesting format screws (which even then tend to have more to them than this), or due to an interesting enough hook and unique enough take to justify giving so little information. I feel like there should be a little more here is all I'm saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was missing the

« SCP-1653 | SCP-1654 | SCP-1655 »

I added it, I hope that is okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is a great jumping of point for a writing challenge. I both want a backstory and recognize that the lack of backstory lends strength to the article, so, a number of options to choose from or reject all of could be the right balance between those things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It reminded me of SCP-2998 because it almost seems as if the Foundation has somehow been taken over or altered in some way. I was half expecting to be some note by a researcher at the end explaining that the Foundation has no record of how this document came into existence.

While much of this procedure could be explained as a desperate measure to save what's left of the world, some of the language used makes the Foundation seem fanatical. The line "Civilians who fail to assist will not know reclamation" is a clear departure from the Foundation's normally clinical tone and almost makes it sound like they're enacting some sort of "Rapture". At the same time, those who are "reclaimed" sound like they're being used up as some sort of resource. We've also never seen the Foundation make blatant use of occult rituals before, and this raises a lot of questions on its own.

Without context, we can't judge whether the Foundation has good intentions, or if they're the real threat and they're attempting to exterminate the human population for some malicious purpose. Hell, we don't even know if this is actually the Foundation, or some other entity posing as them.

I tend to enjoy articles that mess around with the normal SCP format in some way. I like this SCP, but one thing that bugs me is the fact that if this is a "real" document, it doesn't take place at the same time as everything else. I always had the feeling that, although there's no true single SCP cannon, all of the articles are at least meant to exist simultaneously. It's like you're going through a database of top-secret files and all of a sudden one of them is a radio broadcast being sent out during an apocalyptic scenario. It's normally the sort of thing that would be intercepted from an alternate dimension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just barely enough mystery to keep my invested, but once it got me, it dug in. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: uwu >///<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good.

You are a good post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Coming back to this one and comparing it to 579, I think I've identified what makes them sufficiently different (to me).

579's documentation excludes information because you aren't supposed to know it. In-universe, the reader is just as ignorant as you are.

1654's "documentation" excludes information because of course you already know it, what rock have you been living under? Nothing has to get defined or explained because if you're still alive, you already know what's up.

The difference in the text isn't huge but the implications are massively different, and I think they're what make this work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PROTOCOL LAST UPDATED 23993 DAYS AGO

TargetDate = "11/2/1952 12:00 AM";

Interesting. Or coincidence. What's here didn't quite sell the concept for me either way. There are some nice unnerving elements throughout, but they're a bit too disparate for me to leave this feeling much of anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am seconding this. Everything just seem too random for me to put them together and get a hint of what is actually going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I presumed the anomaly was affecting the documentation of it to be a protocol instead of an article, since the Foundation behind it is clearly corrupted by something. That, and it's the only attribute tag that I can affix to this, without a description. It's more like a tale, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why this is meta？

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show thoughts
- I gave 'em in chat and don't want to have to write it all out again :/

8:38 PM <&MaliceAF> Varaxous: yeah no sorry
8:38 PM <&MaliceAF> Varaxous: this could so easily have been really, really cool
8:39 PM <&MaliceAF> but it just comes off as random and slightly anticlimactic to me
8:39 PM <&MaliceAF> there's "leaving the reader wanting more" and there's "not giving the reader enough"
8:41 PM <&MaliceAF> at this point, I'm basically just left feeling annoyed that you filled this niche without really exploring it fully
8:41 PM <&Varaxous> The broadcast or the no description
8:42 PM <&MaliceAF> the idea of a Foundation-to-civilian broadcasted protocol to combat some kind of worldwide threat
8:42 PM <&MaliceAF> there's so much good story potential here, and (to me) it feels like you just skimmed over that in order to make it "teh spooks!!1!"
8:44 PM <&MaliceAF> Varaxous: which would normally be fine, of course, but the concept's so cool and novel I don't think it's being done anywhere near justice
8:44 PM <&MaliceAF> and that missed potential sucks out any enjoyment I could've gotten from this

TL;DR: Downvote, but a sad downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you may have outdone 579, honestly.

Civilians who fail to assist will not know reclamation.

Like, that's a fucking amazing hook. And there's enough description in the procedures to figure out that something has definitely got the Foundation in a frenzy.

But I almost feel like the anomaly here may have compromised the entire Foundation? Or at least whatever part is giving these orders. It would account for the creepy and illogical rearrangement of the sites.

I have a feeling that, if that's not the case, then this is either some infection or the anomaly is along the lines of "human civilization".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding Rogets thoughts, though I don't prefer either over the other. This sort of concept is just something that's interesting once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just weird enough for me, I like it.

Why is it being broadcast on an encrypted channel if civilians are encouraged to help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I prefer 579’s take on this concept, there’s just not enough here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this. The absence of clear information resembles the frequently-reviled SCP-579, and falls into the same traps, but tells a less coherent story without the defense of either novelty or age.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks so much for correcting me. you're my knight in shining armor ♥

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a lot of sound and fury, signifying nothing. It's certainly weird, and the weirdness is of the creepy variety, but it ultimately doesn't come together in any sort of interesting way. All it does is pile weirdness on top of weirdness, without any real point or progression. They don't build on each other, they just kind of keep going until it ends without any resolution (in the broad sense of the term. Things don't have to be explained but the end needs to feel like an end and not just a stop) It doesn't just feel like a story with carefully selected details hidden for the sake of improving tension, it just feels like there isn't any real story. There's too much elided for me to care about imagining the rest. I never get the feeling that there's anything interesting hidden behind this besides "bad spooky shit is happening".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it a whole bunch and I thought it was creative! +1

Also why don't you have an Author Page

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what a beautiful dearth of innovative articles we've had lately.

… you do realize that word means "scarcity" or "lack", right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what a beautiful [WORD MEANING LARGE AMOUNT] of innovative articles we've had lately. i've said it elsewhere and i'll say it here; this site requires injections of boldness and innovation from those daring enough to deny the gospel of format, or we'll wither on the vine. this is an example of precisely that, and you're to be commended for it. well done.

edit: turns out words are really hard when you're as dumb as i am

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I interpreted it less as ridiculous-but-necessary containment procedures enacted by a desperate but rational Foundation, and more that the Foundation has been irrevocably compromised by an entity that compels them to worship it in elaborate and nonsensical ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a line where you can take fear of the unknown so far and still have it work. This skip goes right to the edge of that line and starts pirouetting with it's legs occasionally passing over it

I do like the sense of fear this gives off from all the things that the Foundation must do but to never allow the reader to figure out what the anomaly is feels like they just left the reader at the airport of the city they wanted to tour.

I'll upvote, teetering on a novote

E: Screw that, this is now my favorite SCP. +1 to eternity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the point is that you don't know the actual thing that caused the desperation, Just that this insanity is apparently a valid reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Still lacking the context to judge said desperation against. Something about the whole "shooting reconstituted people bullets from orbit at the SCP until it dies" needs a bit more explanation IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where you read "sinister", I read "desparation". Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this gave me a fritan
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 🤔

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote, mostly due to lack of context.

All I'm getting is that something really bad happened, and that the Foundation effectively goes supervillain to stop it. Without knowing the what or the why to balance it with or judge against, all I'm left with is the heavy levels of sinisterness radiating from the Foundation's actions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some good imagery here, and I'm willing to give the base concept a try, but parts of it feel like they try too hard to be random and disquieting:

(at least 4 mg) of the site director's ocular nerve

their remains are to be integrated into the site food supply.

civilians from the immediate surroundings are to be used.

Should that method be exhausted as well, personnel are instructed to begin creating suitable candidates themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tickles my brain in just the right way, but god I wish there was more. I think that is a good thing, so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The lack of a description is intended.

Wowee. Let's see how this does.
